question:
multiplication (that is, if $a$ and $b$ are in $S$, then so is $ab$).
Let $T$ and $U$ be disjoint subsets of $S$ whose union is $S$. Given
that the product of any {\em three} (not necessarily distinct)
elements of $T$ is in $T$ and that the product of any three elements
of $U$ is in $U$, show that at least one of the two subsets $T,U$ is
closed under multiplication.
solution:
Suppose on the contrary that there exist $t_{1}, t_{2} \in T$
with $t_{1}t_{2} \in U$ and $u_{1}, u_{2} \in U$ with $u_{1}u_{2} \in
T$. Then $(t_{1}t_{2})u_{1}u_{2} \in U$ while
$t_{1}t_{2}(u_{1}u_{2}) \in T$, contradiction.

descriptive_long.question:
multiplication (that is, if $elemone$ and $elemtwo$ are in $bigset$, then so is $elemone elemtwo$).
Let $subsett$ and $subsetu$ be disjoint subsets of $bigset$ whose union is $bigset$. Given
that the product of any {\em three} (not necessarily distinct)
elements of $subsett$ is in $subsett$ and that the product of any three elements
of $subsetu$ is in $subsetu$, show that at least one of the two subsets $subsett,subsetu$ is
closed under multiplication.
descriptive_long.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that there exist $telemone, telemtwo \in subsett$
with $telemone telemtwo \in subsetu$ and $uelemone, uelemtwo \in subsetu$ with $uelemone uelemtwo \in
subsett$. Then $(telemone telemtwo) uelemone uelemtwo \in subsetu$ while
$telemone telemtwo(uelemone uelemtwo) \in subsett$, contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
multiplication (that is, if $moonlight$ and $sandstone$ are in $meadowlark$, then so is $moonlight sandstone$).
Let $cottonseed$ and $nightshade$ be disjoint subsets of $meadowlark$ whose union is $meadowlark$. Given
that the product of any {\em three} (not necessarily distinct)
elements of $cottonseed$ is in $cottonseed$ and that the product of any three elements
of $nightshade$ is in $nightshade$, show that at least one of the two subsets $cottonseed, nightshade$ is
closed under multiplication.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that there exist $driftwood, hazelroot \in cottonseed$
with $driftwood hazelroot \in nightshade$ and $glasswind, riverstone \in nightshade$ with $glasswind riverstone \in
cottonseed$. Then $(driftwood hazelroot)glasswind riverstone \in nightshade$ while
$driftwood hazelroot(glasswind riverstone) \in cottonseed$, contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
multiplication (that is, if $nonmember$ and $outsider$ are in $emptyset$, then so is $nonmemberoutsider$).
Let $openclass$ and $intersection$ be disjoint subsets of $emptyset$ whose union is $emptyset$. Given
that the product of any {\em three} (not necessarily distinct)
elements of $openclass$ is in $openclass$ and that the product of any three elements
of $intersection$ is in $intersection$, show that at least one of the two subsets $openclass,intersection$ is
closed under multiplication.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that there exist $exteriorone, exteriortwo \in openclass$ with $exterioroneexteriortwo \in intersection$ and $interiorone, interiortwo \in intersection$ with $interioroneinteriortwo \in openclass$. Then $(exterioroneexteriortwo)interioroneinteriortwo \in intersection$ while
$exterioroneexteriortwo(interioroneinteriortwo) \in openclass$, contradiction.

garbled_string.question:
multiplication (that is, if $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$ are in $qwerlkjh$, then so is $qzxwvtnphjgrksla$).
Let $asdfghjk$ and $zxcvbnml$ be disjoint subsets of $qwerlkjh$ whose union is $qwerlkjh$. Given
that the product of any {\em three} (not necessarily distinct)
elements of $asdfghjk$ is in $asdfghjk$ and that the product of any three elements
of $zxcvbnml$ is in $zxcvbnml$, show that at least one of the two subsets $asdfghjk,zxcvbnml$ is
closed under multiplication.
garbled_string.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that there exist $slkdmnva, odifjgwe \in asdfghjk$
with $slkdmnvaodifjgwe \in zxcvbnml$ and $plsnrjkw, xvbcnmal \in zxcvbnml$ with $plsnrjkwxvbcnmal \in
asdfghjk$. Then $(slkdmnvaodifjgwe)plsnrjkwxvbcnmal \in zxcvbnml$ while
$slkdmnvaodifjgwe(plsnrjkwxvbcnmal) \in asdfghjk$, contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $S$ be a commutative semigroup (associative, commutative binary operation, no identity required).  Split $S$ into two disjoint subsets $T$ and $U$ whose union is $S$.  Assume that

(1) the product of any six (not necessarily distinct) elements of $T$ lies in $T$, and

(2) the product of any six elements of $U$ lies in $U$.

Prove that at least one of the two subsets $T,\,U$ is closed under multiplication (i.e. is a sub-semigroup of $S$).
kernel_variant.solution:
Suppose, toward a contradiction, that neither T nor U is closed under multiplication.

Step 1.  Because T is not closed, pick t_1,t_2\in T such that their product lies outside T; since T and U are disjoint, we have
   a := t_1 t_2 \in  U.
Likewise, because U is not closed, there exist u_1,u_2\in U whose product lies in T; set
   b := u_1 u_2 \in  T.

Step 2.  Form the common product
   P := a\cdot a\cdot u_1\cdot u_1\cdot u_2\cdot u_2
       = (t_1t_2)(t_1t_2)u_1u_1u_2u_2
       = t_1t_1t_2t_2(u_1u_2)(u_1u_2)
       = t_1^2 t_2^2 u_1^2 u_2^2,
where commutativity lets us rewrite factors freely.

View P in two different ways:
 *  As a,a,u_1,u_1,u_2,u_2 - six individual elements of U.
 *  As t_1,t_1,t_2,t_2,b,b - six individual elements of T.

Step 3.  By hypothesis (2), the first presentation forces P\in U.  By hypothesis (1), the second presentation forces P\in T.

Step 4.  This is impossible because T and U are disjoint.  Hence our initial assumption is false: at least one of the two sets must in fact be closed under multiplication.

Therefore either T or U is a (multiplicatively) closed subset of S, as required.

core_steps: Assume neither T nor U is closed; pick t1,t2 in T with t1t2 in U, and u1,u2 in U with u1u2 in T.
Form the common product P = t1 t2 u1 u2 and view it in two ways: (t1t2)·u1·u2 (three U‐elements) and t1·t2·(u1u2) (three T‐elements).
Invoke the hypothesis that any triple from U (resp. T) has its product in U (resp. T) to get P in U and P in T.
Since T and U are disjoint, this is impossible; hence at least one of the subsets must be multiplicatively closed.

slot1.description: The specific count of factors required in the hypothesis (“three”); any integer k ≥ 3 (repetition allowed) works identically.
